summary: The author developed a 50/50 ensemble of two 3-layer transformer encoders, one attending over symbol_id and the other over time_id with learnable positional encodings. They applied global StandardScaler normalization, zero-filled missing values, and used signed log transformations to stabilize training and capture multiplicative relationships. A key innovation was an online learning strategy that fine-tunes the model on the last 7 days of data daily (7 epochs per day) with a reduced learning rate of 1e-4, which significantly boosted validation scores. Post-competition analysis revealed that extending the training window to 56 days and updating every 8 days yielded further improvements.
extensive_summary:
## Model Architecture & Training Setup
The solution relies on a 50/50 ensemble of two 3-layer transformer encoders. The first encoder applies attention over all `symbol_id` tokens, while the second applies attention over all `time_id` tokens using learnable positional encodings. To maintain training stability and prevent dead neurons, GELU activations were chosen over ReLU, SiLU, and PReLU. The model was trained to predict all 9 responders simultaneously, which improved both training stability and generalization. Optimization used AdamW with gradient clipping and model EMA.

## Feature Engineering & Preprocessing
The feature set consists of 79 numeric features (`feature_00` to `feature_78`), `time_id`, and `weight`. To stabilize training and better capture multiplicative relationships, the author applied signed log transformations to the features. Missing values were simply filled with 0. Global normalization was performed using sklearn's `StandardScaler`.

## Online Learning Strategy
The primary score driver was a carefully tuned online learning (OL) pipeline. Initial attempts at single-step updates on the latest day yielded minimal gains. Through validation experiments on the last 20-30 days, the author discovered that fine-tuning on a recent window of data significantly improved CV scores for future days. The final OL strategy involved training on the last 7 days of data every day, effectively applying 7 epochs per new day. The learning rate was reduced to 1e-4 (from the original 1e-3) while keeping other hyperparameters identical. Post-competition analysis revealed that extending the training window (e.g., last 56 days) and reducing update frequency (e.g., every 8 days) provided an additional ~0.0012 score improvement, highlighting the importance of window size and update cadence in OL.
competition_flow: Raw tabular time-series data is normalized with StandardScaler, missing values are filled with zeros, and fed into a 50/50 ensemble of two 3-layer transformer encoders that are continuously fine-tuned daily on the most recent 7 days of data using a reduced learning rate to produce predictions for 9 responders.
data_processing: Signed log transformations applied to features for training stability and to better learn multiplicative relationships in log space
features_engineering: signed log transformations applied to features
models: 3 layers transformer encoder with attention over symbol_id
3 layers transformer encoder with attention over time_id with learnable positional encoding
ensembling: The final submission uses a 50/50 ensemble of the two transformer encoder variants, with online learning applied daily via fine-tuning on the most recent 7 days of data at a learning rate of 1e-4.
insights: Using GELU activations prevents dead neurons and maintains parameter utility during online learning compared to ReLU, SiLU, or PReLU.
Predicting all 9 responders simultaneously improves training stability and generalization.
Fine-tuning on a recent window of validation data significantly boosts future-day scores, establishing a foundation for the online learning strategy.
critical_findings: Single-step online updates on the latest day of data barely improve the score, whereas fine-tuning on a multi-day window yields significant gains.
Reducing the learning rate to 1e-4 during online learning is critical for stability and performance compared to the original 1e-3.
Retraining on a longer historical window (e.g., last 56 days) less frequently (e.g., every 8 days) provides a more substantial score boost than daily updates on a shorter window (last 7 days).
what_did_not_work: Single update step on the latest day of data given by the API barely helped.
